metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace AC4's `grep -c` proving command with one that asserts ≥1 match **per file**, e.g. `for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do grep -qE "committer's own configured git identity|configured git identity" "$f" || { echo "FAIL: $f missing required phrase"; exit 1; }; done`.
evidence: 'grep -c … reports at least one match in each of the three files' — `grep -c` exits 0 as long as the pattern is found in at least one file; per-file zero counts do not raise the exit code
No downstream assertion (awk, xargs, shell arithmetic) is prescribed to check each per-file count independently
findings.finding: AC4's proving command (`grep -c ... | reports at least one match in each of the three files`) does not fail automatically when a file has zero matches — `grep -c` with multiple files prints per-file counts and exits 0 even when one file shows `filename:0`, so the command cannot serve as a binary gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 verify command: `grep -c "committer's own configured git identity\|configured git identity" CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md`
scenarios: Executor updates AGENTS.md and rebar.md but forgets CONTRIBUTING.md; grep -c shows CONTRIBUTING.md:0 but exits 0; CI stays green; AC is declared done even though CONTRIBUTING.md never got the required language
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
for f in CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md; do
  grep -qE "committer's own configured git identity|configured git identity" "$f" \
    || { echo "FAIL: $f missing required phrase"; exit 1; }
done
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the `'docs/**/*.md'` git pathspec in the AC5 consistency-check grep command with a truly recursive form (e.g. `'docs/'` with `--include='*.md'` via `git grep -r`, or use the `:(glob)docs/**/*.md` magic pathspec prefix) and verify it matches files in docs/adr/, docs/design/, and docs/calibration/ before declaring the check complete.
evidence: Plan Remediation: 'scanning AGENTS.md, .agents/rules/*.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, docs/**/*.md (recursive, so it also covers docs/adr/, docs/design/, docs/migrations/, docs/calibration/, etc.)'
AC5: verify command uses pathspec `'docs/**/*.md'`
docs/ directory confirmed to contain: adr/, design/, migrations/, calibration/, research/, experiments/, archive/, assets/, licenses/ subdirectories — all would be silently missed

git grep behavior: ** in a pathspec is NOT treated as a recursive glob without :(glob) magic prefix or --glob-pathspecs flag; on most git versions 'docs/**/*.md' either matches only docs/<subdir>/*.md (one level deep) or is treated as a literal
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the AC5 consistency-check grep pathspec `'docs/**/*.md'` is recursive and 'also covers docs/adr/, docs/design/, docs/migrations/, docs/calibration/, etc.' but git grep does NOT expand `**` as a recursive glob by default — a bare `'docs/**/*.md'` pathspec matches only files named `*.md` in an immediate subdirectory of docs/ (i.e., `docs/X/*.md`), not deeper nesting, and on many git versions matches nothing at all. The docs/ directory is confirmed to have the named subdirectories (adr/, design/, migrations/, calibration/, research/, experiments/, etc.), so the claimed recursive coverage is empirically false for the prescribed command.
findings.location: Remediation section (consistency-check grep command) and AC5 verify command: `git grep -nE '...' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md'`
scenarios: A contributor-facing doc under docs/adr/ or docs/design/ reintroduces 'Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' — the prescribed lint check passes silently, providing false confidence that the sweep is clean.
The implementer runs the prescribed git grep command during verification and it exits 0 even with bot identity strings present in docs/adr/*.md.
findings.suggested_fix:
Use `git grep -rn --include='*.md'` (dropping the pathspec glob in favor of `-r` + `--include`) or use the magic pathspec `:(glob)docs/**/*.md`. Example corrected command:
```
git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' \
  -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md ':(glob)docs/**/*.md'
```
Alternatively, invoke `grep -r --include='*.md'` over `docs/` as a standard shell command rather than a git pathspec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change the AC5 git grep pathspec from `'docs/**/*.md'` to the :(glob)-prefixed form `':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` (or equivalently use `--glob='docs/**/*.md'`) so recursive ** expansion is guaranteed by git's pathspec engine regardless of shell or git version.
evidence: Plan text: 'scanning AGENTS.md, .agents/rules/*.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, docs/**/*.md (recursive, so it also covers docs/adr/, docs/design/, docs/migrations/, docs/calibration/, etc.)'

git grep pathspec behavior: without the :(glob) magic prefix, ** is not guaranteed to recurse into subdirectories on all git versions; 'docs/**/*.md' as a literal pathspec to git grep may match only top-level docs/ .md files depending on git version and globbing settings
docs/ directory listing confirms subdirectories adr/, design/, migrations/, calibration/ exist and contain contributor-facing .md files
findings.finding: The AC5 lint grep command uses the unquoted-glob pathspec 'docs/**/*.md' passed to git grep. Without the :(glob) pathspec magic prefix, git grep does not guarantee recursive ** expansion across all git versions — it may silently match only files directly under docs/ rather than the subdirectories the plan explicitly names (docs/adr/, docs/design/, docs/migrations/, docs/calibration/). The plan asserts recursive coverage of these subdirectories in the same sentence, creating a mismatch between stated scope and the literal command.
findings.location: Remediation section and AC5 (the git grep command: `git grep -nE '...' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md'`)
scenarios: The lint check is added with 'docs/**/*.md' as written; on a git version where ** is not recursive in pathspecs, the check silently misses docs/adr/*.md, docs/design/*.md, etc.; a reintroduced hardcoded identity in those subdirectories would not be caught.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace `'docs/**/*.md'` with `':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` in both the Remediation grep command and the AC5 verify command, e.g.:
```
git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' \
  -- AGENTS.md ':(glob).agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md ':(glob)docs/**/*.md'
```
This uses git's built-in glob pathspec magic, which guarantees recursive `**` semantics across all git versions ≥ 1.9.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `git grep` negative-pathspec syntax (e.g., `':!docs/experiments/**'`) needed to exclude `docs/experiments/` from the lint check, so the implementer can wire the exclusion correctly without guessing.
evidence: Plan grep template: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md'`
AC5: 'explicitly excluding `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, and `docs/experiments/`'

Confirmed via search: docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/operator_attested_ac_corpus.jsonl, docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e6_selfconsistency_inputs.jsonl, and many harness scripts contain `joeoakhart` strings
The negative pathspec syntax (e.g., `':!docs/experiments/'`) needed to implement the exclusion in git grep is absent from the plan
findings.finding: The lint check mechanism has an unresolved exclusion gap: AC5 specifies scanning `docs/**/*.md` (recursive) while simultaneously listing `docs/experiments/` as an intentional exclusion, but never specifies the `git grep` syntax to implement that exclusion. The `docs/experiments/` tree contains hundreds of `joeoakhart` hits (corpus JSONL, harness scripts with `/Users/joeoakhart/` paths, eval files referencing the bot email). A naive implementation of the prescribed grep template — without a `:!docs/experiments/` negative pathspec or equivalent — will permanently fail the lint gate, making the consistency check unusable.
findings.location: Remediation section (lint check grep command) and AC5
scenarios: Implementer follows the AC5 grep template verbatim; `make lint` fails permanently due to `docs/experiments/` corpus hits, making the regression guard ship broken.
Implementer manually adds `docs/experiments/` exclusion but uses shell glob exclusion syntax instead of git pathspec syntax, causing silent non-exclusion under `git grep`.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the grep template in both the Remediation section and AC5 to: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md' ':!docs/experiments/**'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite AC3's proving command to fail non-zero when any match falls outside the allowed prefixes, e.g. pipe through `grep -vE '^(infra|.github/workflows|tests|docs/experiments)/'` and assert the result is empty.
evidence: 'returns matches ONLY under infra/, .github/workflows/, tests/, or docs/experiments/; any match outside those prefixes fails the check' — but the command as written doesn't filter or fail on out-of-scope matches
No pipe to grep -v, awk, or shell exit-code logic is prescribed to enforce the exclusion automatically
findings.finding: AC3's proving command (`git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'`) lists all matches repo-wide but provides no mechanism to fail when a match appears outside the allowed prefixes — an executor must manually inspect output and judge, making the check non-deterministic.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 verify command: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'` … returns matches ONLY under allowed prefixes
scenarios: A match slips into docs/adr/some-file.md; git grep prints it; executor sees mixed output but the command exits 0; the AC is marked done without catching the violation
findings.suggested_fix:
```bash
git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' \
  | grep -vE '^(infra/|\.github/workflows/|tests/|docs/experiments/)' \
  | { read line && echo "FAIL: out-of-scope match: $line" && exit 1 || exit 0; }
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the `git grep` pathspec for `docs/` subdirectories uses `:(glob)docs/**/*.md` (or equivalent `--` with explicit recursive glob support) rather than bare `docs/**/*.md`, and verify the chosen form actually matches files in `docs/adr/`, `docs/design/`, and other named subdirectories before integrating into `make lint`.
evidence: Plan states: 'scanning `AGENTS.md`, `.agents/rules/*.md`, `CONTRIBUTING.md`, `docs/**/*.md` (recursive, so it also covers `docs/adr/`, `docs/design/`, `docs/migrations/`, `docs/calibration/`, etc.)'
The prescribed command: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md'`
git pathspec `**` without `:(glob)` magic is NOT guaranteed recursive across git versions — MISMATCH between asserted behavior and standard git pathspec semantics
Evidence class: Partially-verified — git grep itself exists and is used, but the specific `docs/**/*.md` recursive-coverage surface is Unverified/Contradicted by known git pathspec behavior

Repo `docs/` has deep subdirectories: `docs/adr/`, `docs/design/`, `docs/calibration/`, `docs/migrations/`, `docs/research/` — any of which could reintroduce the hardcoded identity and be silently missed by a non-recursive pathspec
findings.finding: The plan prescribes `git grep -nE ... -- 'docs/**/*.md'` and explicitly asserts this pathspec recursively covers `docs/adr/`, `docs/design/`, `docs/migrations/`, `docs/calibration/`, etc., but `git grep` does NOT treat `**` as a recursive glob without the `:(glob)` pathspec magic prefix; without it, the pathspec may silently fail to match files in subdirectories on many git versions, leaving the consistency check incomplete.
findings.location: Remediation section (git grep command) and AC5 verify step
scenarios: A contributor re-introduces the hardcoded identity in `docs/adr/0015-dco.md`; the lint check runs `git grep ... -- 'docs/**/*.md'` but the glob does not recurse into `docs/adr/`, so the check exits 0 — the regression is silently accepted.
On a git version where `**` in an unquoted pathspec is shell-expanded before git sees it, the behavior differs from a version where git handles it natively, causing inconsistent CI vs local results.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `'docs/**/*.md'` with `':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` in the git grep command, which explicitly enables pathspec glob magic and guarantees recursive subdirectory matching regardless of git version. Alternatively, use `$(git ls-files 'docs/**/*.md')` or a `find docs/ -name '*.md'` pipe into `xargs grep` to achieve portable recursive coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either prescribe the exact required phrase in the remediation section (so the executor knows what to write) or relax AC4's grep pattern to match the conceptual requirement (e.g. `grep -qE 'git config user\.(name|email)|configured.*git identity|committer.*identity'`).
evidence: Remediation: 'Make the canonical guidance identity-neutral: contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor performing the commit' — no prescribed wording
AC4 grep: `grep -c "committer's own configured git identity\|configured git identity"` — requires one of two exact substrings to be present in each file
findings.finding: AC4 mandates a specific literal phrase ('committer's own configured git identity') in CONTRIBUTING.md and the other two files via grep, but the remediation section only says to 'describe the concept' — an executor who writes semantically correct but differently worded guidance will satisfy the intent yet fail the proving command, creating an unresolvable conflict.
findings.location: Remediation section ('Make the canonical guidance identity-neutral') vs. AC4 verify command
scenarios: Executor rewrites CONTRIBUTING.md with 'use the identity registered in your local git configuration' — semantically equivalent but the AC4 grep returns 0 for that file, signalling failure even though the remediation goal is met
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Remediation section: 'When updating the files, include the sentence: "Sign off using the committer's own configured git identity (`git config user.name` / `git config user.email`)."' — then the AC4 grep pattern matches deterministically.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC #4's verify grep with the wording the Remediation actually prescribes: either add the required phrase to the Remediation body, or relax the grep to match the `git config user.name`/`user.email` language already specified in AC #1 and the Remediation.
evidence: Remediation: 'contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor performing the commit (`git config user.name` / `git config user.email`)'
AC #4 verify: '`grep -c "committer's own configured git identity\|configured git identity" CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md` reports at least one match in each'
The Remediation never specifies 'committer's own configured git identity' as required wording; AC #1 only requires `git config user.name`/`user.email` to appear in AGENTS.md
findings.finding: AC #4's verify step requires the verbatim phrase `"committer's own configured git identity"` or `"configured git identity"` to appear in CONTRIBUTING.md, AGENTS.md, and .agents/rules/rebar.md, but the Remediation body never prescribes this exact wording — it only instructs contributors to reference `git config user.name`/`git config user.email`. An implementer following the Remediation's description faithfully may write equivalent but differently worded guidance and fail the AC #4 grep.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #4 verify step; Remediation section
scenarios: Implementer writes 'use your configured git identity (git config user.name / git config user.email)' in all three files — satisfies the Remediation and AC #1 but fails AC #4's grep, creating a false failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC #4's verify grep to `grep -c 'git config user\.name\|git config user\.email' CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md .agents/rules/rebar.md` (matching the terminology the Remediation actually mandates), and remove or make optional the 'committer's own configured git identity' phrase requirement.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.

verify command `grep -q 'git config user.name' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'git config user.email' AGENTS.md`.` greps the unanchored word 'git' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_git'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"git":')

verify command `grep -q 'git config user.name' AGENTS.md && grep -q 'git config user.email' AGENTS.md` greps the unanchored word 'git' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_git'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"git":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction in AC #3: either add `CLAUDE.local.md` to the exclusion list in AC #3's verify step and AC #5's lint grep, or remove `CLAUDE.local.md` from the list of acceptable locations in AC #3's prose.
evidence: AC #3 text: 'e.g. `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `CLAUDE.local.md`'
AC #3 verify: 'returns matches ONLY under `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, or `docs/experiments/`' — CLAUDE.local.md absent
AC #5 lint check excludes only 'infra/, .github/workflows/, tests/, and docs/experiments/' — CLAUDE.local.md absent
Cross-section contradiction: AC #3 body permits CLAUDE.local.md; AC #3 verify and AC #5 would flag it as a failure
findings.finding: AC #3's text names `CLAUDE.local.md` as an acceptable location for retained bot identity, but AC #3's own verify step and AC #5's lint check both exclude only `infra/`, `.github/workflows/`, `tests/`, and `docs/experiments/` — not `CLAUDE.local.md`. A match in `CLAUDE.local.md` would therefore pass the AC text but fail the verify step and the lint check, sending the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #3 (text vs. verify step) and AC #5 (lint exclusion list)
scenarios: Implementer retains bot identity in CLAUDE.local.md as allowed by AC #3's prose, then AC #3's own verify grep fails, and the lint check fails — contradictory outcome from a single AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `CLAUDE.local.md` to the exclusion pattern in both the AC #3 verify grep and the AC #5 lint grep (e.g., `-- ':!CLAUDE.local.md' ':!infra/' ...`), or drop it from AC #3's example list if it is not truly a sanctioned location.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consolidate the bot-identity-absence checks: designate one AC as the single authoritative negative check covering all in-scope files (AGENTS.md, .agents/rules/*.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, docs/**/*.md minus exclusions), remove the overlapping greps from the other ACs, and rely on AC #5's lint check as the canonical enforcement mechanism.
evidence: AC #2 verify: `! git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- '.agents/rules/*.md' AGENTS.md`
AC #3 verify: `git grep -nE '...'` returns matches ONLY under exclusion prefixes (covers all files project-wide)
AC #4 verify: `git grep -l 'Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- CONTRIBUTING.md AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md'` returns no results
All three verify the same negative condition (no bot identity in guidance files) with different but overlapping file sets and patterns
findings.finding: ACs #2, #3, and #4 are near-duplicate in verifying that bot identity is absent from guidance files, with three overlapping grep commands covering substantially the same files and strings, creating redundancy that risks false confidence (a gap in one is obscured by the others) and contradictory pass/fail states.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC #2, AC #3, AC #4 verify steps
scenarios: A reviewer checks ACs #2 and #4 against CONTRIBUTING.md — AC #2 doesn't cover CONTRIBUTING.md, so a residual match there passes AC #2 silently but fails AC #4, making it unclear which AC owns CONTRIBUTING.md.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extract the hardcoded bot-identity patterns (`joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`, `Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart`) into a single named constant or comment-annotated variable in the script/Makefile target, and add a one-line note specifying where to update the pattern when the bot identity changes.
evidence: Plan prescribes `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'` as the literal check pattern with no reference to a named constant, config variable, or update procedure.
If the bot identity changes (e.g., account rename, org change, new automation actor), the lint check silently becomes stale and the regression it guards against is no longer detected.
No update-procedure or 'change this string here' callout is included in the plan.
findings.finding: The proposed lint grep hardcodes the literal string `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` (and `Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart`) directly in the Makefile target or script with no mechanism to update it if the bot account is renamed or replaced. This is a rule-expected-to-evolve that is asserted as a static constant, violating the T5e changeability sub-criterion.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Add a deterministic consistency check' paragraph; AC item 5
scenarios: Bot account is renamed from joeoakhart+bot to a team account; old hardcoded string in the grep never fires; the identity-neutral contract is silently eroded by a future commit.
A second bot identity is added; the grep misses it because no pattern-extension procedure was documented.
findings.suggested_fix: In the script or Makefile target, define a shell variable or Make variable at the top (e.g., `BOT_IDENTITY_PATTERN='joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart'`) with an adjacent comment: `# Update this pattern if the automation account identity changes`. Use the variable in the grep call. This keeps the rule in one place and makes evolution explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for the `make lint`-integrated grep approach (e.g., 'consistent with how actionlint and zizmor are already wired into `make lint`; runs in CI under the same gate that blocks submission') so implementers understand the design intent.
evidence: Plan states what to implement but not why this mechanism fits: 'a make lint-integrated grep step (e.g. a target in the Makefile, or a script under scripts/ invoked from make lint)'
No sentence explaining the choice relative to alternatives
Existing codebase precedent: tests/unit/test_ci_actionlint_scope.py uses a pytest guard to verify make lint invokes actionlint — a structurally different enforcement pattern already present
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for why a `make lint`-integrated grep step was chosen as the enforcement mechanism rather than alternatives (a pytest-based Makefile guard, similar to `tests/unit/test_ci_actionlint_scope.py`, or a pre-commit hook). The codebase alignment is discoverable from the Makefile but is not articulated in the plan itself.
findings.location: Remediation section, 'Add a deterministic consistency check' paragraph
scenarios: An implementer choosing between a Makefile target and a pytest guard has no stated rationale to guide the decision, and may choose inconsistently with project conventions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the updated AGENTS.md (and `.agents/rules/rebar.md`) a concrete fallback decision rule specifying what an agent/contributor should do when `git config user.name` or `git config user.email` is blank — e.g. 'if either is unset, run `git config user.name "Your Name"` and `git config user.email "you@example.com"` before committing; do not proceed with an empty identity' — so the agent instruction is self-contained and does not assume a pre-configured environment.
evidence: AC1 requires AGENTS.md to tell contributors/agents to 'verify git config user.name and git config user.email and to sign off with that configured real identity' — but specifies no fallback when those values are missing.

AGENTS.md:162 currently reads: 'Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart <joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com> (add with git commit -s)' — a fully self-contained instruction an agent can execute without any environment prerequisite.

The plan's Remediation says 'contributors and agents use the real name and email configured for the actor performing the commit (git config user.name / git config user.email)' but does not specify what an agent does when git config is unset.

docs/identity-setup.md:50 shows that rebar itself already handles the case where git config user.email may differ from the identity email, but AGENTS.md does not point agents to this setup guidance for the pre-commit case.
findings.finding: The plan instructs agents to sign off using 'git config user.name' / 'git config user.email' but specifies no fallback or decision rule for the common agent-environment case where these git config values are blank or unconfigured (e.g. a fresh worktree in a CI sandbox or a newly provisioned agent environment). The current AGENTS.md:162 gave agents a concrete exact string; the replacement removes that concrete value but leaves agents with no protocol for what to do when the git-config lookup returns empty — a pink-elephant gap where the negative ('don't use Joe's identity') is clear but the positive decision tree is incomplete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC1 / Remediation section / AGENTS.md:162 (current instruction being replaced)
scenarios: A coding agent (Codex, Claude Code, Copilot) spins up in a fresh sandbox where git config user.name and user.email are not pre-set; the updated AGENTS.md tells it to 'use the configured identity' but gives no instruction for the empty-config case, so the agent either stalls, generates a malformed sign-off, or falls back to a default (possibly an incorrect one).
A first-time external contributor clones the repo before setting up git identity; the updated guidance says 'check git config' but doesn't say what to do if it's wrong or missing.
findings.suggested_fix: In the replacement guidance, add a two-line prerequisite check block immediately before the sign-off instruction: 'Before signing, confirm your identity is configured: `git config user.name` and `git config user.email` must both return non-empty values. If either is unset, configure them now (`git config user.name "Your Name"` / `git config user.email "you@example.com"`) — a missing or empty identity will cause Gerrit to reject the push.'

findings.finding: AC4 has no concrete executable proving command; it describes the desired consistency condition in prose ('all three name... the committer's own configured git identity... and none of them names a specific third party') but provides no grep, script, or structured check an agent can run to verify it in-session.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC4: 'Verify: `grep -q "git config user.name" CONTRIBUTING.md && grep -q "git config user.name" AGENTS.md && grep -q "git config user.name" .agents/rules/rebar.md`' — matching the style of AC1's Verify clause.

findings.finding: The plan's AC5 consistency-check grep command uses the git pathspec `'docs/*.md'`, but this pathspec does NOT recursively match .md files in docs/ subdirectories (e.g. docs/adr/*.md, docs/design/*.md, docs/migrations/*.md). The docs/ directory has confirmed subdirectories containing .md files. As written, the grep step would silently pass even if a hardcoded bot identity were reintroduced into any subdirectory-level contributor-facing documentation, defeating the stated purpose of the deterministic check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC5 / Remediation section — grep command: `git grep -nE '...' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/*.md'`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `'docs/*.md'` with `'docs/**/*.md'` in both the Remediation grep command and the AC5 verification command. Alternatively, use `git grep -rE '...' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md docs/` to cover all .md files under docs/ recursively, then add `--exclude-dir` or pathspec exclusions for docs/experiments/.

findings.finding: The AC5 grep pathspec `'docs/*.md'` is non-recursive and will silently miss all contributor-facing Markdown files in docs/ subdirectories (docs/adr/, docs/design/, docs/migrations/, docs/calibration/, docs/research/, etc.). The plan's own Cascade flag says to verify 'any other contributor-facing guidance under docs/', which contradicts the shallow glob.
findings.location: AC5 (consistency check grep command) and Remediation section (grep command)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the pathspec in both the Remediation grep command and AC5 verify command from `'docs/*.md'` to `'docs/**/*.md'`. For git-grep specifically, use `git grep -nE '...' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/**/*.md'` or equivalently add `':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` as the pathspec.

findings.finding: The AC5 grep command uses `'docs/*.md'` as a git pathspec, which in git grep is non-recursive and matches only files directly under `docs/` — it silently misses all contributor-facing docs in `docs/` subdirectories (e.g. `docs/adr/*.md`, `docs/design/*.md`, `docs/archive/*.md`, `docs/migrations/*.md`) that are not in the explicitly excluded `docs/experiments/`. If the hardcoded bot identity string reappears in any such subdirectory, the lint check passes with no signal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC5 — the git grep command `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' 'docs/*.md'`
findings.suggested_fix:
Use: `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' 'CONTRIBUTING.md' ':(glob,exclude)docs/experiments/**' ':(glob)docs/**/*.md'`
Or, if using a scripts/ Python helper (consistent with the existing check_docs_index.py pattern), use Python's `pathlib.Path('docs').rglob('*.md')` with an explicit filter to skip `docs/experiments/`.

findings.finding: The consistency-check grep command uses the glob `docs/*.md`, which in standard shell and git-grep does NOT match files in subdirectories of `docs/`. Because the plan explicitly identifies `docs/experiments/` as an exclusion (proving subdirectories exist), other contributor-facing docs nested under `docs/` (e.g. `docs/adr/`, `docs/contributing/`) would silently escape both the sweep and the lint guard, allowing the hardcoded identity to persist undetected.
findings.location: Remediation section (grep command) and AC item 5
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git grep -nE 'joeoakhart\+bot@navapbc\.com|Signed-off-by: Joe Oakhart' -- AGENTS.md '.agents/rules/*.md' CONTRIBUTING.md ':(glob,exclude)docs/experiments/**' ':(glob)docs/**/*.md'` (git pathspec syntax), and update AC5 to match.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
